speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Robert Cooper, Birmingham, Alabama. This is tape number 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, we were talking about Kansas City. How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was in Kansas City for about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] more than two years. It's a wonderful place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a-- that's another story. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In-- at the end of 1950, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was drafted. I, I received my draft papers. And I wasn't a citizen yet. But you, you don't have to be a citizen of this country to be drafted into the Army. That was during the Korean conflict.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was drafted. And after a few machinations and tests, since we wanted to win the Korean War, I was sent to Germany as an interpreter. So this was my German homecoming, which wasn't nearly as bad as my last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about going back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fantastic. Much better than going to Korea. Nobody was shooting at you in Germany at, at that time. And I was, I was made a, a cadre, which is a, a, sort of a [NON-ENGLISH], as an, as an interpreter. And I was an acting sergeant. And I was in charge. I was helping train European enlistees into the United States Army from behind the Iron Curtain countries, like Romanians, Hungarians, Poles, Russians, Czechs. And I quite enjoyed my two years in Germany. I didn't achieve any high rank, but then I was a little too freewheeling and not enough regimented. But I enjoyed my stay in Germany. And then I was-- some people were trying to talk me into re-enlisting. And I looked at them as if they were crazy. I, I don't much like a regimented life. I don't like for anybody to tell me what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And so then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came back to this country and got married. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, let's do it chronologically.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back to this country, to New York. And I wanted to have some formal schooling. So with the help of my GI Bill of Rights, I entered New York University. And I almost finished. And then I got married. I married a girl from Brooklyn. She was introduced to me by an uncle of mine who had come to New York also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Barbara Weiner, W-E-I-N-E-R. And she is the mother of my three children. And from Kansas, from-- we worked in New York. I ran a quilting factory. And she was a bookkeeper. And I was also going to school part-time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the names of your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest one is David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1958 here in Birmingham. The next one was Sherry. And she was born in 1960. And the next one was Mark, and he was born in 1962. They're two years apart. And there's an addition to that. When my children were born, the boys in particular, I insisted that they have names where they can drop the Aizenman, in case they don't want to be known as the Aizenmans from Przytyk. Perhaps, perhaps they didn't-- they, they would want names that don't identify them as Jews. I mean, I was, I was paranoid, rightly so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my first son, David, I gave him the name David Burton, which is actually a takeoff on my father's name, Baruch. So if he wants to drop it-- if he wanted to drop the name Aizenman, his name, he could be David Burton, like Richard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my other son, Mark, I found a good name for him. I like Jeffrey. So his middle name is Jeffrey. And if he wanted to drop the Aizenman, fine. I wasn't that hung up on my name. Anyhow, none of my kids have dropped the Aizenman, but I always told them they weren't too smart. [LAUGHS] Anyhow, my--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to Birmingham?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's, that's also an interesting story. I was working, I was working as a, a-- I was running a quilting factory in New York. And there were some demands I made of my bosses that they wouldn't want to, didn't want to comply with.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some friends of mine in Kansas City suggested that I go to work for the jewelry company [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and become a traveling salesman. So my wife and I said, let's try it. So we went to Kansas City. And there was about a six-month period of training.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they were going to send me as a traveling salesman to Minnesota, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but-- and I had already bought my long underwear and everything. But they found out that the salesman who lived in Memphis, who traveled in the South in Alabama and Mississippi, had resigned. They asked me if I wanted to take this place. And I'd heard of-- I'd heard nothing good about Birmingham in those days. But I said, I'll try it. And Birmingham was actually the center of my territory. So when I came to Birmingham-- after giving away our long underwear-- and we arrived on January 1, 1958, there was a blizzard in Birmingham, without long underwear. And I started traveling very intensively. And I've, I've always found the South to be a very congenial place. I think the South is one of the nicest places in the United States. It's still undiscovered. People think of the South, particularly of Birmingham, as a, a, a redneck Mecca. I, I disagree. There, there, there are rednecks all over the country. I think that there's fewer-- there are fewer feelings against the Jews in the South than in other places that I've seen in this country. And I've, I've traveled around.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, did you run into anti-Semitism in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost, almost negligible, almost none. Maybe, maybe I was only comparing it to the anti-Semitism in Europe--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where, where it was rampant.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I now-- I have three grandchildren. Oh, now-- excuse me-- four now. Last month, another grandchild was born. My, my oldest son, David, is a physician. And he now has three children. And my other son, who has been a chef-- now he sells meat to supermarkets and stores-- he has one child, a little girl. And when my kids were growing up, they would ask me questions about, about my time in camps. And I had a very tough time communicating with them. So I was very flippant about it. I'm looking forward to getting one of these tapes, so they can sort of realize more or less what it was, even though sometimes when I give a talk, my children show up. Some of them do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you give talks often now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that often, but whenever I'm asked in a school, a middle school or a grade school, at the drop of a hat, I, I, I give talks. But mostly, I answer questions. And I only do it because a friend of mine was telling me that it's the only way you can get through to certain kids. I am the one whom they are going to remember. And the connotation is going to be he's a survivor. He was in a concentration camp. It's not all a story that they heard someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one last question concerning the whole story. Why do you think you survived? A lot of people did not. Have thought about that at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I were religious, I could say because God wanted me to survive. But since I'm not religious, I'd say it was a, a toss-up. Maybe I was mean enough to survive. But I would say mostly it's a, a coincidence or an accident. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, people ask me-- some of my friends, in particular, ask me why are you always in such a good humor? I say, I-- every birthday that I have is a new experience for me. I mean, I have actually lived more than-- the last May the 2nd was my 51st born-again birthday. I have lived at least 51 years longer than I expected to or that anybody else ever expected me to. Why shouldn't I'd be happy? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a great life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would, I would like to add that it's a wonderful thing that you are doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Have you any pictures to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just very few. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father when he was a young man. It's on the grave of Theodor Herzl, my-- the founder of Zionism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is the one on the very bottom. He was a young man of 19 or 20 in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the only picture I have of my parents after they were married. This is 1933. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they were both in their 20s. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And this is the only picture I have of my family, my maternal grandparents, my aunts. And I am the little kid sitting on the very bottom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you point out each individually with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My, my father, my mother, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] my grandmother and grandfather. These are my aunts and uncle. And this one was a fiance of one of my aunts. Now, out of this picture, there are three people, four people who survived. Now there are three people left.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] OK. Now we'll do the--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're ready. I can see your papers. See what we got here. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Henry, would you like to introduce your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, it's my pleasure. This is Sherry, my wonderful daughter. She's a masseuse. This is Mark, my wonderful youngest son. My oldest one didn't show up. Ask them anything you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Just that-- either one of you, did your father share these experiences with you when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I remember the first time he really ever talked much about the concentration camps, I was a young teenager. When I needed a history project, he came in really handy right around at that point in time. So maybe junior high school age or so. Before then, there was a lot of information given by our mom, maybe about why Henry did certain things the way he did and reacted and responded the way he responded to certain situations in our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I say it on tape? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- I think there was just personality conflicts in general regarding parenting and for me, specifically, I guess my needs from a parent-- to have a parent present, for example. Henry was traveling my whole young life. And when he would be at home, he was pretty strict around us. And he wanted things exactly his way. And there wasn't too much talking him out of his way. And I always-- I know I learned the art of bargaining exactly from Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I guess Mom explained to me in the best way she could that Henry did not have the type of parenting that other people had. And so his examples as-- from a parent to give as a parent were not, for example, like my friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so for me, that was probably the most difficult part of growing up. But he's already told me that he refuses to be guilty for it. And I can finally accept that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you, Mark? Did-- when did you learn about these experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a little different for me, being the youngest. And from what I remember, it took a while for my dad to come out with the stories and to answer the questions and to be as open as he is now. Being younger, I-- it's not that I wasn't interested. I just didn't really realize-- and my schooling didn't really tell us what happened during the Holocaust. After I got a little older, I got out of high school, went in the military, off to Germany, then it started to kick in as to what really happened. And then I started to ask more of the questions. And, and he was starting to come out with it. He wrote a little biography that I read that really put a light on the situation as to what the reality was and what he went through as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do either one of you think about your father participating in this project and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's about, it's about time. We've been, been kind of chiding him about documenting his history for, for the grandchildren, if nothing else. And we've given him a pen and paper. And he's been given a tape recorder. And he's been telling his story for so long to so many people that it only makes sense, because he's living history, you know, that his part of history is documented.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it takes a lot of pressure-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it takes a lot of pressure off a lot of people, mostly Henry. It really takes a lot of pressure off him, because he says he's not really good at writing anyway. So I think this is a-- this is a good thing. And Henry, is there any words of wisdom you would like to share with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, oh. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Stay out of concentration camps. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, actually, the, the, the thing that I would like to tell my children is don't let anybody tread on you. If you, if you see any part of the government or authority trying to abrogate your freedom, fight against it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You are your own person. You're a free person. Use your freedom, and use it wisely. And give your children more slack than I've given mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why do you think you were-- I mean, did your experiences in these camps influence your parenting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure. My, my experience with the camps influenced me, influenced my whole life. It's, it's not-- we are the sum of our experiences. At least, I know I am. Some of them I've tried to overcome. And some of them I have overcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: But generally, I, I would like to have changed some of my actions. But you can't go back in time. Maybe you can. Who knows? But it's never, it's never too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like to know which ones, because that's-- that's a question I've never really gotten to ask is if you could have specifically done something different as a parent, what would--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have, I would have treated you much better, Sherry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. So much for 10 years of psychotherapy. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Whose? Yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love him anyway.
